title: 2025/08/25 - EW Subcommittee A [Part 4]
displayTitle: 2025-08-25 - Education and Workforce Committee - Employment Relations Amendment Bill [Subcommittee A, Part 4]
description: Monday 25 August 2025 | Education and Workforce Subcommittee A Employment Relations Amendment Bill Submitters: Coal Action Murihiku (CAM) Group - Jenny Campbell Dan Mills Te Runanga o Nga Toa Awhina, NZPSA - Virgil Iraia, President – Public Service Association - Mike Tana, Kaitohutohu Kaupapa Here Māori Women's Network of the PSA - Alix Crosbie, Women's Committee Member - Sonja Lister, Women's Committee Member - Kirsten Windelov, Policy Advisor Gail Duncan Workers First Union, Inc. - Anita Rosentreter, Strategic Project Coordinator Coromandel Workers Council - Pamela Grealey
descriptionSummary: Employment Relations Amendment Bill. Coal Action Murihiku (CAM) Group. Jenny Campbell

Um, the proposed employment relations amendment bill, specifically its introduction of an income threshold for unjustified dismissal presents a significant policy shift against worker protections.

Um, an analysis of the regulatory impact statement, um, on this matter from January this year, reveals that the change is poorly targeted, carries economic risks, and is likely to undermine job security without clear benefits to the economy. The core rationale for the threshold is to simplify dismissal processes for employees with a significant impact on organizational performance and high bargaining power.

Um, however, the government's chosen model an automatic income threshold set at 180 K fundamentally fails. Many individuals in the targeted bracket are not senior executives, but are technicians, trade workers, senior engineers, IT specialists, and medical professionals. Uh, these individuals, while they're while compensated, they don't necessarily have the outsized influence and negotiation attributed to A CEO, nor do they possess sophisticated bargaining power to negotiate individual protections in their agreements.

Um, the consequences of this poor targeting is the creation of non mutually beneficial arrangements. RIS itself warns of the risk of dismissing employees who otherwise would have been successfully performance managed at a reasonable cost. It introduces a new layer of inefficiency, um, and poor outcomes for protections from employees. Furthermore, the policy overlooks the variable resilience of high income earners.

The RAS notes that financial resilience increases with income, though some high income households would struggle over a sudden loss of earnings, particularly if there was a single income supporting larger households. The erosion of job security as a direct and negative knock on effect on the economy. Secure employment is a primary driver of consumer confidence and discretionary spend.

Professionals on high incomes are significant contributors to the domestic economy through expenditure on housing, hospitality, retail, and local services. Um, introducing uncertainty into their employment status might incentivize greater financial portion. Um, this could manifest as reduced discretionary spending, um, and in an economic environment that requires robust domestic consumption, uh, policies that potentially suppress spending among top earners are counterproductive.

DIS also highlights the likelihood of unintended legal consequences. With the path of unjustified dismissal, blocked employees and their representatives will naturally seek out other avenues for challenge. RIS anticipates An increase in breach of contract claims, the use of bespoke private dispute resolution processes. This simply shifts the compliance costs and legal uncertainty from one area of the law to another.

Rather than eliminating it. It may also lead to a more complex and protracted litigation, uh, contrary to the bill stated aim reducing red tape, uh, just like you to guys to consider the behavior that might manifest as a result of the legislation. Some employees be incentivized to cap their income with reduced hours, reducing productivity and the supply of the central services, or move into consulting, essentially charging higher rates for the same organization, um, which is gonna further increase costs and exacerbate the brain drain on

important roles in our economy. So in conclusion, the government's chosen model for the amendment is a blunt instrument that captures a wide SWAT of the workforce by whom the policy rationale does not apply. It trades proven employment protections for an uncertain legal landscape, and by undermining the job security of high units at risk stamping the very consumer confidence and discretionary spending that are vital to economic growth.

For these reasons, the amendment's unsound and I urge the committee to re-look at the RAS and consider damaging the scope to senior management roles only, um, or asking people to research more thorough the implications to all parties rather than just the rushed anecdotal evidence. Um, in particular though, uh, I think, uh, it's this body's role to continue to protect the rights of the most vulnerable and the contractual arrangements and provide a more balanced approach to all parties, not just employers. I'd like to talk to you about what I've seen in my working life as a PSA delegate, the impact of the 90 day rule on employees, both in terms of feeling insecure in their jobs and fear of changing jobs as they might be unemployed after 90 days.

It was very evident from 2013 that removing the 30 days in which an employment contract can be reviewed against a collective employment agreement, left new employees very much in the dark about their employment agreement rights, the introduction of a new class of worker specified contractors who have no holiday pay, sick leave, maternity leave, parental leave, family violence, leave to name a few, no retirement savings, no minimum wage criteria specified, and will themselves be responsible for a CC levies and other criteria could open the doors to exploitation,

particularly of those in contracted casual work and those with multiple contract jobs and drive wages to the bottom and tendering for contracts. The justification for this bill is to support greater market flexibility and going for growth to deliver higher productivity and wages. This bill will only deliver reduced wages, lower opportunities and conditions, and bring unnecessary hardship and stress to workers, including the loss of access to justice.

Just as women workers have been undercut with the equal pay employment, equal pay amendment bill and 12.8 billion has been withdrawn from the economy in terms of wages forfeited. So a further downgrading of future investment and spending by workers will be disabled by this bill.

Workers won't be able to pay taxes spend and investors they could have, which reduces the tax for the take for the government, the Bill of roads, workers' rights, and will reduce their future earning prospects, causing a spiraling downward system leading to a poorer economic outcome for New Zealand. This bill permits a tremendous degree of power on the, on the part of the employer to impose specified contractor status on employees which could lead, which will lead to forced misclassification.

Overall, the bill will lead to unaddressed poor working conditions and exploitation, which will in turn impact on productivity. The bill is going in the wrong direction, undoing the fair labor laws that New Zealand is founded on passing of this bill will inflict harm due to the asymmetry of power between the employer and the employee.

It won't reduce red tape and compliance as a contracting arrangements proposed are in fact, more complex and sacrificing workers' rights will not lead to growth. To conclude, I recommend the Select Committee abandon abandon this bill in entirely and go back to the drawing board, defining the issue, consulting more widely to address all employment and business concerns while respecting the very important process of unions and protecting worker rights.
